title: Harness Engineering — Evaluation & Benchmarks
description: Harness engineering is the practice of building evaluation pipelines to measure LLM performance systematically. Without a harness, you are guessing — with one, you can optimize prompts, models, and systems with data.

sections.title: The Evaluation Harness
sections.description:
An evaluation harness is a standardized framework for running models against benchmarks and collecting metrics. The gold standard is EleutherAI's LM Evaluation Harness — the same framework used to produce the official numbers for LLaMA, Mistral, and Falcon. It supports 200+ benchmarks and 100+ model architectures. Understanding it is essential for any ML engineering role.

Key concepts:
• Task: A benchmark (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K) with its dataset, prompt template, and metric.
• Model: The LLM being evaluated, loaded via HuggingFace transformers.
• Few-shot: Number of in-context examples. Standard: 0-shot, 5-shot for most benchmarks.
• Metric: Accuracy, exact match, F1, BLEU, pass@k, etc.

Why harness matters: Without a harness, you can't: (1) Compare models objectively, (2) Measure if your prompt changes helped, (3) Catch regressions after fine-tuning, (4) Justify model choices in design reviews.
sections.codeBlock:
# EleutherAI LM Evaluation Harness — running a benchmark
# pip install lm-eval
# Run from command line:
# lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf \
#         --tasks mmlu --num_fewshot 5 --device cuda:0 --batch_size auto

# Programmatic usage
# import lm_eval
# from lm_eval.models.hf_causallms import HFLM
# from lm_eval import evaluator

# model = HFLM(pretrained="meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")
# results = evaluator.simple_evaluate(
#     model=model,
#     tasks=["mmlu", "gsm8k", "hellaswag"],
#     num_fewshot=5,
#     batch_size=8,
#     limit=100  # limit tasks to N samples for quick iteration
# )
# print(f"MMLU accuracy: {results['results']['mmlu']['acc']:.3f}")

# Custom task definition (YAML format)
# In a file like tasks/my_task.yaml:
task_config = """
task: custom_reasoning
dataset_path: json
dataset_name: null
test_split: test
doc_to_text: "Question: {{question}}\nAnswer:"
doc_to_target: "{{answer}}"
metric_list:
  - metric: exact_match
    aggregation: mean
    higher_is_better: true
repeats: 1
num_fewshot: 3
"""
# ponytail: real custom tasks use HuggingFace datasets or JSON files

sections.title: Standard Benchmarks: What They Measure
sections.description:
Understanding what each benchmark actually tests is critical for interpreting numbers and choosing the right eval for your use case:

MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding): 57 subjects across STEM, humanities, social sciences. 4-choice multiple choice format. 5-shot. Tests world knowledge and reasoning breadth. Most widely reported single metric. Ceiling ~90% for GPT-4o/Claude 3.5.

GSM8K (Grade School Math): 8,500 grade-school math word problems. Tests arithmetic reasoning. Requires chain-of-thought. Models score 0-shot without CoT (they guess), ~90% with CoT for top models.

HumanEval: 164 Python function completion problems with unit tests. Tests code generation. Metric: pass@k (k samples, percentage where at least one passes all tests). pass@1 for GPT-4o ~87%.

HellaSwag: Commonsense reasoning — choose the most plausible ending to a scenario. Tests physical commonsense. Near-saturation for modern models (~95%).

BIG-Bench (Beyond the Imitation Game): 204 tasks covering logic, math, physics, social reasoning, etc. Tests emergent abilities. The benchmark where scaling effects were most clearly demonstrated.

MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark): 8 tasks, 58 datasets. For embedding models only. Averages: classification, clustering, pair classification, re-ranking, retrieval, STS, summarization, and bitext mining.

sections.title: LLM-as-Judge Evaluation
sections.description:
For subjective metrics (helpfulness, safety, coherence), you need a judge LLM to evaluate responses. This is the standard approach used by Chatbot Arena (Elo ratings from human preference), LMSYS, and most production eval pipelines.

Protocols:
• Single-answer grading: Judge evaluates one response against a rubric. Fast, but the judge has a bias toward its own style of response (model collusion).
• Pairwise comparison: Judge sees two responses (A and B) and chooses which is better + explains. The gold standard used by Chatbot Arena. Mitigates position bias by swapping A/B positions and averaging.
• Reference-based: Judge evaluates the response against a gold-standard reference answer. Better for factual tasks.

Judge selection: Use a different model (e.g., GPT-4o judges LLaMA, not LLaMA judging itself). Fine-tuned judges (Pandas, UltraRM) offer better alignment with human preferences than raw use of frontier models.

sections.title: Continuous Evaluation in Production
sections.description:
Benchmark scores are necessary but not sufficient — you must also evaluate on production data continuously. Standard pattern:

1. Log all inputs and outputs: Every production LLM call is logged (query, response, latency, token count, user feedback if available).

2. Sample for evaluation: Run an LLM judge on a random 1–5% sample of production traffic using task-specific rubrics: helpfulness, factuality (if you have ground truth), safety, instruction following.

3. Regression detection: Track scores over time. Any change of >1% triggers an alert. Common causes: model update, prompt change, data distribution shift, upstream API change.

4. A/B testing: Deploy a new prompt or model variant to 10% of traffic. Compare production metrics (user satisfaction, task success rate, latency, cost) against the control group.

The golden test set: Curate 100–500 production examples with human-annotated ground truth. This is your regression detection suite. Run it on every candidate change before deploying.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• EleutherAI LM Evaluation Harness — The industry-standard framework for evaluating LLMs, used at FAANG for model benchmarking
  • MMLU: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding (Hendrycks et al., 2021) — The most widely used LLM benchmark
  • HellaSwag: Can a Machine Really Finish Your Sentence? — Commonsense NLI benchmark with adversarially generated wrong answers
  • GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners — Original GPT-3 paper with evaluation methodology
  • A Careful Examination of Large Language Model Performance on Grade School Arithmetic — Critical analysis of GSM8K evaluation methodology

details:
Benchmark contamination: A model trained on internet data may have seen the test set during pre-training. This artificially inflates scores. Mitigations: (1) Use benchmarks with held-out test sets (MMLU-Pro, GSM1K). (2) Check for n-gram overlap between training data and test samples. (3) For internal models, create bespoke benchmarks from your proprietary data that the model couldn't have seen. (4) Always report the benchmark version and contamination check results.

FAANG interview tip: When asked 'How would you measure the quality of this LLM feature?' — always propose layered evaluation: (1) Offline benchmarks for quick iteration, (2) Golden test set for regression detection, (3) Production A/B testing for real-world impact, (4) User satisfaction surveys for subjective quality. Few engineers mention all four layers — doing so signals ML engineering maturity.
